plan: Step 1: Create occluder fragment shader (`src/visual/shaders/occluder.frag.glsl`). Minimal shader — outputs any color since colorWrite:false prevents pixel output. Just `gl_FragColor = vec4(0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0);`. Register in shaderRegistry.ts.

Step 2: Create instanced occluder vertex shader (`src/visual/shaders/occluderInstanced.vert.glsl`). Extends the wireframe.vert.glsl deformation pipeline with InstancedMesh support: reads `instanceMatrix` (auto-provided by THREE.js InstancedMesh), applies instance transform before the standard deformation chain (radial scale → bass expansion → bass noise → twist → drift → slow modulation → pointer repulsion → field spread → breath). Uses the same uniforms as wireframe.vert.glsl. Computes aRandom from world-space position using the hashRandomFromPosition pattern (sin/cos coefficients 73.1/91.3/117.7 etc.) since instanced geometry can't carry per-vertex aRandom. This produces approximate-but-sufficient deformation matching for depth occlusion.

Step 3: Extend `cubeGrowth.ts` generator to export instance transforms. Add to `CubeGrowthOutput`: `instanceOffsets: Float32Array` (vec3 per cube), `instanceScales: Float32Array` (float per cube), `normScale: number` (the normalization factor applied). After the BFS loop builds `instances[]` and before merging into vertex buffers, compute the normalization scale. Apply it to instance offsets and scales so they match the normalized vertex positions. This data feeds the InstancedMesh occluder.

Step 4: Extend `cubeLattice.ts` generator to export alive cell transforms. Add to `CubeLatticeResult`: `cellOffsets: Float32Array` (vec3 per alive cell, post-centering), `cellScale: number` (the cell size value). These are derived from the existing cell-alive mask and grid layout. Only alive cells get entries, matching the rendered wireframe topology.

Step 5: Create occluder utility module (`src/visual/occluder.ts`). Exports: (a) `createOccluderMaterial(vertexShader, uniforms)` — returns ShaderMaterial with `colorWrite:false, depthWrite:true, depthTest:true, transparent:false, side:THREE.FrontSide, polygonOffset:true, polygonOffsetFactor:1, polygonOffsetUnits:1` (polygon offset avoids z-fighting with coplanar edges/vertices). (b) `createInstancedCubeOccluder(offsets, scales, normScale, uniforms)` — creates InstancedMesh from unit BoxGeometry, sets instanceMatrix per cube from offset+scale data, applies the instanced occluder vertex shader + occluder fragment shader, sets renderOrder=-1. (c) `syncOccluderUniforms(occluderMesh, sourceUniforms)` — copies time/audio/pointer/deformation uniforms from edge material to occluder material each frame.

Step 6: Add occlusion to `wireframePolyhedra.ts`. Add `enableOcclusion?: boolean` to `WireframePolyhedraConfig`. When enabled: (a) For each polyhedron, create a solid Mesh using `createBaseGeometry(shape, radius)` (the existing function in polyhedraEdges.ts — reuse THREE.js built-in geometries: IcosahedronGeometry, OctahedronGeometry, etc.). (b) Apply occluder material with the standard wireframe vertex shader (same as edges, NOT instanced — each polyhedron is a different shape). Attach matching aRandom attribute computed from vertex positions via hashRandomFromPosition. (c) Set same position/rotation as the paired edge/vertex meshes. (d) Set renderOrder=-1 on occluder, renderOrder=0 on edges/vertices. (e) Change edge and vertex material to `depthTest:true` (keep depthWrite:false, keep transparent:true, keep AdditiveBlending). (f) Extend MeshPair to include optional `occluder: THREE.Mesh`. (g) Update draw() to sync occluder uniforms. (h) Update cleanup() to dispose occluder.

Step 7: Add occlusion to `fractalGrowthWireframe.ts`. Add `enableOcclusion?: boolean` to `FractalGrowthConfig`. When enabled: (a) Use instance transforms from cubeGrowth output to create an InstancedMesh via `createInstancedCubeOccluder()`. (b) The InstancedMesh uses a unit BoxGeometry with 8 vertices / 12 triangles. Each instance's matrix encodes the cube's offset and uniform scale (post-normalization). (c) Occluder uses the instanced vertex shader which computes aRandom from position hash. (d) Set renderOrder=-1 on occluder, renderOrder=0 on edges/vertices. (e) Change edge and vertex materials to depthTest:true. (f) Update draw() to sync occluder uniforms. (g) Update cleanup() to dispose occluder InstancedMesh.

Step 8: Add occlusion to `cubeLatticeWireframe.ts`. Add `enableOcclusion?: boolean` to `CubeLatticeConfig`. When enabled: (a) Use alive cell transforms from cubeLattice output to create an InstancedMesh via `createInstancedCubeOccluder()`. Each alive cell is one cube instance at its grid position with cellSize scale. (b) Same instanced vertex shader + occluder fragment shader. (c) Same renderOrder, depthTest, uniform sync, and cleanup pattern as fractalGrowth.

Step 9: Wire occlusion toggle through quality tier system. In `quality.ts`, add `enableOcclusion: boolean` to quality profiles: off for 'low' tier (save GPU), on for 'medium' and 'high'. In `main.ts` (or wherever system configs are assembled), pass quality.enableOcclusion into each wireframe system's config. This satisfies the 'can be toggled per geometry system' acceptance criterion since each system has its own enableOcclusion flag derived from but overridable vs. the quality default.

Step 10: Update `shaderRegistry.ts` to register occluder and occluderInstanced shader attribute requirements. The occluder standard shader needs `position` + `aRandom`. The instanced shader needs only `position` (aRandom is computed in-shader). Register both so the health gate validates them.

Step 11: Write tests. (a) `occluder.test.ts`: verify material properties (colorWrite:false, depthWrite:true, depthTest:true, transparent:false, polygonOffset:true), verify InstancedMesh creation with correct instance count, verify uniform sync copies all required uniforms. (b) `wireframePolyhedra.us070.test.ts`: with enableOcclusion:true, verify occluder Mesh created per polyhedron, verify matching position/rotation with edge mesh, verify edge material has depthTest:true, verify occluder renderOrder < edge renderOrder, verify cleanup disposes occluder. (c) `fractalGrowthWireframe.us070.test.ts`: with enableOcclusion:true, verify InstancedMesh created with correct cube count, verify edge depthTest:true, verify renderOrder, verify cleanup. (d) `cubeLatticeWireframe.us070.test.ts`: same pattern as fractalGrowth but for lattice cells. (e) Generator tests: verify cubeGrowth returns instanceOffsets/instanceScales/normScale, verify cubeLattice returns cellOffsets/cellScale.

Step 12: Build verification and visual QA. Run `npm run build` to confirm no TypeScript errors. Load in browser and verify: (a) wireframe shapes with occlusion show proper hidden-line removal (back edges hidden), (b) shapes remain readable and 3D-looking, (c) deformation/audio-reactivity still works on visible edges, (d) no shader compile errors in console, (e) toggling occlusion off restores see-through wireframe look, (f) performance is acceptable (check frame time with and without occlusion via debug overlay).
testing_recommendations: Unit test occluder material factory: assert colorWrite===false, depthWrite===true, depthTest===true, transparent===false, polygonOffset===true, polygonOffsetFactor===1, polygonOffsetUnits===1, side===FrontSide
Unit test InstancedMesh creation: assert instance count matches cube/cell count from generator, assert instanceMatrix entries are set (not identity), assert renderOrder===-1

Unit test uniform sync: assert all deformation uniforms (uTime, uBassEnergy, uTrebleEnergy, uMotionAmplitude, uPointerDisturbance, uPointerPos, uCadence, uBreathScale, uNoiseFrequency, uRadialScale, uTwistStrength, uFieldSpread, uDisplacementScale, uEnableSlowModulation, uEnablePointerRepulsion) are present on occluder material and updated in draw()
Unit test per-system toggle: create each wireframe system with enableOcclusion:false, verify no occluder meshes added to scene; create with enableOcclusion:true, verify occluder meshes present
Unit test edge/vertex materials with occlusion enabled: assert depthTest===true, depthWrite===false (unchanged), transparent===true (unchanged), blending===AdditiveBlending (unchanged)
Unit test renderOrder: occluder.renderOrder < edges.renderOrder AND occluder.renderOrder < vertices.renderOrder
Unit test cleanup: after cleanup(), verify occluder geometry.dispose() and material.dispose() called, verify occluder removed from scene
Integration test cubeGrowth generator: verify new output fields (instanceOffsets, instanceScales, normScale) are present and have correct lengths (3*cubeCount for offsets, cubeCount for scales)
Integration test cubeLattice generator: verify new output fields (cellOffsets, cellScale) are present and cellOffsets length matches alive cell count * 3

Visual regression test: load each wireframe mode (polyhedra, fractalGrowth, cubeLattice) with occlusion enabled, verify no shader compile errors in console, verify back-face edges are hidden, verify front-face edges are visible and colored correctly
Performance test: compare frame time with occlusion on vs off — the instanced occluder for cubeGrowth (up to 160 cubes at high tier) should add minimal overhead (single draw call via InstancedMesh)
Shader compile test: verify occluder.frag.glsl and occluderInstanced.vert.glsl compile without errors via the health gate system
risks: Deformation mismatch between instanced occluder and merged wireframe: The instanced occluder computes aRandom from position hash, while cubeGrowth/cubeLattice wireframes use sequential PRNG-based aRandom. This means the deformation won't be pixel-perfect identical. For occlusion purposes this is acceptable (the occluder just needs to roughly match the wireframe volume), but extreme bass deformation could cause visible edge bleeding or premature occlusion. Mitigation: keep the polygonOffset bias, and optionally scale the occluder slightly (0.98x) to be conservative.

Z-fighting on coplanar edges: Wireframe edges that lie exactly on the surface of the occluder solid may flicker due to depth-buffer precision limits, especially at distance. Mitigation: polygonOffset on the occluder material pushes solid faces slightly behind edges in depth, and depth fog already fades distant geometry.

Additive blending + depth test interaction: Currently wireframes use AdditiveBlending which accumulates light. With depthTest:true, overlapping transparent edges behind the occluder are correctly hidden, but the visual character changes from 'glowing see-through' to 'solid 3D wireframe'. This is the intended artistic change but may need tuning of opacity/fog to compensate for the darker look (fewer additive contributions).

Performance on low-end devices: Adding an occluder pass doubles the vertex processing for wireframe systems (occluder vertices + edge vertices). For cubeGrowth at high tier (~160 cubes = 1280 triangulated cube vertices), the InstancedMesh keeps this to one extra draw call. But low-tier devices may still struggle, hence occlusion is disabled for the 'low' quality tier.

THREE.js InstancedMesh with custom ShaderMaterial: THREE.js automatically provides `instanceMatrix` as an attribute on InstancedMesh, but custom ShaderMaterial must explicitly reference it. If the vertex shader doesn't use `#include <begin_vertex>` / `#include <project_vertex>` chunks, we must manually multiply by instanceMatrix. This requires careful GLSL authoring and testing across browsers.

Electric arc mode compatibility: wireframePolyhedra has an optional electric arc mode with subdivided edges and arc-specific shaders. The occluder doesn't need arc deformation (it uses the standard wireframe deformation), but the visual match between arc-displaced edges and the standard-deformed occluder could cause some edges to peek through or be prematurely hidden. This is an acceptable tradeoff since arc displacement is small-scale lateral movement.
